heading: MODULO DECIMO
card_summary: Chunk 120 forms the tenth "module" of Zorzi's Cantico Terzo and develops an elaborate theology of the celestial banquet (convivio) that awaits the blessed at the heavenly nuptials. Zorzi treats the bread, wine, flesh, and fruit served at this eschatological feast through sustained typological exegesis, drawing on John 6, the Wedding at Cana, the Last Supper, Ezekiel's river-vision, and the Apocalypse. Jewish rabbinic tradition is cited for the heavenly banquet's three courses (bull, bird, fish), while the twelve fruits of the Tree of Life from Revelation 22 are mapped onto the twelve apostles and the twelve zodiacal signs. The section concludes with Isaiah 65's vision of the new Jerusalem, the wolf and the lamb eating together, and the serpent's permanent expulsion from the feast, all read as descriptions of the final state of perfect peace and union with Christ.
essay:
# MODULO DECIMO: The Celestial Banquet — Bread, Wine, Flesh, Fruit

## Overview and Central Argument

This module constitutes an extended meditation on the eschatological feast prepared for the blessed at the heavenly nuptials (*nuptiae coelestes*). Zorzi proceeds through five *Concentus* (harmonic movements), each treating a different element of the banquet: the bread, the wine, the flesh, the fruits of the Tree of Life, and the abundance and sweetness of the heavenly food. The controlling metaphor throughout is the transformation (*transmutatio*) of the communicant into the body of Christ — a Eucharistic logic extended to its ultimate eschatological fulfillment.

---

## Concentus I: The Bread

Zorzi opens by noting that God as "ottimo Artefice" and "ottimo Provveditore" has prepared food for both dimensions of the human being — animal and spiritual. For the animal dimension: bread and wine. For the spiritual, bread too, but a heavenly bread called *manna* and identified with Christ himself:

> Io sono il pane vivo disceso dal cielo, se uno ne mangerà, vivrà in eterno.

<cite>p. 2605 / f. 443v (John 6:51, cited in Italian translation)</cite>

The eucharistic logic here is Augustinian: the ordinary bread is transformed into the body of the eater, but the heavenly bread reverses the process — the eater is transformed into the bread. Augustine is explicitly cited on this point. The goal is full incorporation into Christ so that "tutti noi, che partecipiamo di un solo pane e di un solo calice siamo un corpo solo e un pane solo" (1 Cor 10:17). The Latin text of Concentus I closes with the image of the *mana absconditum* (Rev 2:17) that "nemo scit, nisi qui accipit" — unknowable except through grace-given experience.

---

## Concentus II: The Wine

The wine of the heavenly wedding is pressed from the True Vine (Christ, John 15:1) and was foreshadowed at the Wedding at Cana. Zorzi offers a careful allegorical reading of the six stone jars (*hydriae sex*), citing Jerome's commentary on Ezekiel: the six jars represent the six ages of the world as vessels capable of holding both supramundane and lower waters. When water passes through Christ the True Vine and is warmed by the *supramundanus sol*, it becomes good wine. If, however, the jars contain only lower water (mere gratuitous graces without supernatural orientation) and are heated by the fire of *concupiscentia*, they produce the wine of Sodom — the "vinum" cursed by Moses in Deuteronomy 32:32-33:

> De vineis Sodomorum vinea eorum, et de suburbanis Gomorrae. Uva eorum uva fellis, et botrus amarissimus. Fel draconum vinum eorum, et venenum aspidum insanabile.

<cite>pp. 2608-2609 (Deut 32:32-33, cited in Latin)</cite>

Zorzi then introduces the twofold wine that must be drunk on earth as preparation: the wine of *caritas* and the wine of *sapientia*. The latter is distinguished from the higher wisdom (called *lac* / milk elsewhere in the work) and is available to many, not just to a few. The Pentecost event is read as a divine inebriation: Peter at the Transfiguration, Paul's desire to "dissolvi et esse cum Christo," Stephen's martyrdom made sweet by inebriation — all are instances of this wine tasted in advance. The Greek term *Parakletos* (Paraclete / Consoler) is glossed as the name of this wine "quod vere laetificat cor hominis." The wine is also identified with the Blood of the Lamb poured out in the Eucharist.

> Et ille est verus Bacchus, et verus Sol.

<cite>p. 2611 (Zorzi's identification of Christ as the true Bacchus and the true Sun)</cite>

The phrase "verus Bacchus et verus Sol" is characteristic of Zorzi's Neoplatonic-Christian synthesis, fusing solar theology and Dionysian ecstasy into a Christ-centered framework.

---

## Concentus III: The Bread Made Flesh

Zorzi distinguishes the bread-become-flesh of the Eucharist from the other "meats" served at the heavenly banquet. Drawing on the parable of the Great Banquet in Matthew 22 ("Tauri mei et altilia occisa"), he cites Jerome's interpretation: bulls (*tauri*) symbolize the grandeur of dogmas, and fatted birds (*altilia*) the heights of divine doctrine. More significantly, Zorzi appeals to Jewish rabbinic wisdom (*Hebraei sapientes*) for the tradition that God prepares for the just a banquet of three courses: a bull, a bird, and a fish — representing respectively the animal dimension of the soul (purified by the legal bull sacrifice), the supreme intellectual part of the soul that ascends like an eagle, and the rational/intermediate spirit nourished in the waters of wisdom.

> I sapienti ebrei, invece, affermano che Dio prepara per i giusti un banchetto in cui saranno serviti un toro, un uccello e un pesce.

<cite>p. 2614</cite>

The fish is then given a remarkable exegesis via the post-Resurrection meals in Luke 24 and John 21. The risen Christ eats "pesce arrostito e favo di miele" and declares its "avanzi" (reliquiae) to be the words he spoke while among humans. The spiritual words of Christ are therefore the true fish of the heavenly banquet — relics of the Spirit that must be preserved with even greater reverence than the bodily relics of the saints.

The key to eucharistic transformation is identified as the "fire of the Holy Spirit and of love," without which no digestion and transmutation occurs. Here Zorzi cites, alongside Platonic Academicians and Epicureans, above all "Dionysius noster" (Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite) for the principle that love transforms the lover into the beloved.

---

## Concentus IV: The Fruits of the Tree of Life

This is the most technically elaborate section. Zorzi harmonizes Ezekiel 47:12 (the river, the perennial fruit trees, leaves for medicine) with Revelation 22:1-2 (the Tree of Life bearing twelve kinds of fruit monthly, leaves for the healing of nations). The twelve monthly fruits are decoded as the twelve apostles corresponding to the twelve zodiacal signs through which the sun marks the twelve months:

> i mesi in quelle dimore eterne non si riferiscono alla scansione temporale ma alla ripartizione distributiva dello spazio, raffigurata dalla scansione temporale di quaggiù come le cause dagli effetti o le forme autentiche dalle raffigurazioni pittoriche.

<cite>pp. 2618-2619</cite>

The 144,000 sealed from Revelation 7 (the "dodicimila" of each tribe) are invoked as the primary members of these twelve apostolic families. The twelve fruits are the apostles themselves as primary food of the whole family of the blessed. The "leaves for healing" are identified either with preachers who serve others rather than themselves, or with the sacraments — typified by Adam's inadequate fig leaves, which could not cover his nakedness because they were not leaves of the Tree of Life. The full contrast is drawn: Adam was prevented from taking from the Tree of Life (Gen 3:22) because the time had not yet come; but with the fullness of time (*plenitudo temporis*), the sacramental leaves are freely distributed. All twelve fruits ultimately depend on the single foundation of Christ Jesus, the Tree irrigated by the river flowing from the Sanctuary.

---

## Concentus V: Abundance and Sweetness

Zorzi closes with a meditation on the abundance (*copia*) and sweetness (*suavitas*) of the heavenly food, anchored in Psalm 144:7 ("Memoriam abundantiae suavitatis tuae eructabunt"). A technical philological note is offered: the Hebrew root *naba'* (נָבַע) means both "to abound" and "to belch/overflow," and Zorzi prefers the translation "rigurgiteranno" (they will belch forth) over "parleranno" (they will speak), following the Septuagint and Augustine. The spiritual gluttony of the feast is praised in contrast to the condemned gluttony of carnal food. Isaiah 65's prophecy of the new Jerusalem is read as a description of the final state: servants will eat and drink while the wicked hunger and thirst. The wolf and the lamb feeding together (Isa 65:25) signifies the reconciliation of justice and mercy at the Last Judgment. The serpent, permanently expelled, is left only dust to eat — the punishment assigned at Genesis 3:14. The module closes with the eschatological promise: "Non nuoceranno né uccideranno su tutto il mio monte santo," the final state of unbroken peace and union with Christ.

---

## Sources and Authorities

- **Scripture**: John 6, John 2 (Cana), John 15, 1 Cor 10, Deut 32, Ps 75, Ps 22, Ps 33, Ps 107, Isa 65, Ezek 47, Rev 22, Luke 24, John 21, Lam (Jeremiah), Isa 38 (Hezekiah)
- **Church Fathers**: Augustine (on eucharistic transformation, on Ps 144:7, on the blessed state); Jerome (on Ezekiel, on the six jars and six ages, on the eagle as the supreme part of the soul)
- **Pseudo-Dionysius** ("Dionysius noster"): the principle that love transforms the lover into the beloved
- **Jewish tradition**: *Hebraei sapientes* on the three-course heavenly banquet (bull, bird, fish)
- **Classical**: implicit reference to Platonic Academicians and Epicureans on love's transformative power
- **Hebrew philology**: Zorzi parses the root *naba'* (נ-ב-ע) with its vocalized form *yabbi'u* (יַבִּיעוּ) to support the reading "eructabunt"

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This module fits within the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s macro-structure as a Canticum-level treatment of the soul's ultimate destiny. The eucharistic and eschatological themes here develop the work's sustained Neo-Platonic doctrine of *henosis* (union) translated into Christian sacramental terms: the multiplication of souls into the single body of Christ mirrors the Neoplatonic return of the Many to the One. The zodiacal mapping of the apostles and the identification of Christ as "verus Bacchus et verus Sol" typify Zorzi's confident synthesis of pagan cosmological imagery with Christian revelation, characteristic of the Venetian Franciscan Observant milieu in which he worked.
